title: 48" Open-Center Bathroom Vanity
summary: A 48" vanity cabinet with an open center shelf cut for plumbing, flanked by a door on one side and a drawer on the other — a full vanity cabinet, not a hutch that bridges over an existing one.
description:
This is a complete plywood vanity carcass built from one sheet ripped into 20-1/4"-wide strips, with two enclosed end sections and an open shelf in the middle — sized around the plumbing rather than around it. **Cut the center shelf's back edge to clear the supply lines and drain before you build it in**, since that cutout has to be sized to your actual plumbing, not a fixed dimension.

Build the plywood carcass first with 3/4" pocket holes, then build the face frame separately — this two-piece approach lets you square the face frame on a flat bench before it ever touches the carcass, which is much easier than framing directly onto an already-assembled box. Attach the face frame to the carcass with finish nails and glue once both are done.

**One side gets a drawer, the other a door** — build the drawer to fit its actual opening and slides rather than to a fixed dimension, since plywood thickness and face frame reveal both shift the real opening size slightly from the plan. The drawer box is inset 3/4" from the face frame to leave room for the drawer front, and the shaker-style door is built from 1x3 stiles and rails around a 1/2" plywood center panel. **Fit the doors and drawer front to your own openings last**, after the carcass and face frame are both actually assembled, rather than cutting them to the plan's numbers up front.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 20-1/4" strips, full sheet
Paint, primer or stain and poly

cutList.note: Cut the back edge to clear plumbing before installing.

steps.title: Cut the carcass, frame and door parts
steps.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 20 1/4"-wide strips and crosscut two sides 35", the bottom 46 1/2", two inner dividers 31 1/2", two end shelves 12 1/2", and the centre open shelf 20". Check your sheet count before you start: those seven parts need about 224 1/2" of 20 1/4"-wide strip, and a 4x8 sheet yields only two full-length strips, or 192" — roughly 32 1/2" short, so plan on a second sheet or another source for the last parts. From the 1x2 cut two front-to-back nailers 20 1/4", two face frame stiles 35", two top and bottom rails 45", two centre shelf face stiles 5 3/4", and two door and drawer opening stiles 22 3/4". From the 1x4 cut two short back supports 12 1/2", the centre back support 20", and the kick rail 45". Cut four door stiles 16" and four door rails 9 3/4" from the 1x3, and two door panels 10 3/4" x 16" from the 1/2" plywood. Leave the drawer box stock uncut — it gets sized to the opening you end up with.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 20-1/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Build the carcass
steps.body: Stand the two 35" sides on the 46 1/2" bottom — 46 1/2" plus the two 3/4" sides is the vanity's 48" overall width, so confirm that number before driving a screw. Set the two 31 1/2" inner dividers between them so the interior reads 12 1/2", divider, 20", divider, 12 1/2": that adds to exactly 46 1/2", which is how you know the dividers are where they belong. Fit the two 12 1/2" end shelves into the outer bays at the heights you want. Drill 3/4" pocket holes and fasten everything with 1 1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, then measure both diagonals across the assembled box and rack it square before the glue sets.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 20-1/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Fit the center shelf
steps.body: Hold the 20" centre shelf up to your actual plumbing and mark the supply lines and the drain on its back edge, then cut those notches before the shelf ever goes in — the cutout is sized to your rough-in, not to a number on a plan. Cut generously: a notch slightly too big disappears behind the trap, one slightly too small means pulling the shelf back out after the face frame is on. Install it fixed with pocket holes, or drill shelf-pin holes in both dividers if you would rather it move later.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 20-1/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Add the back supports and nailers
steps.body: Fasten the two 12 1/2" and one 20" 1x4 back supports across the back of the carcass, one per bay, flush with the back edges of the sides and dividers — their lengths match the bay widths exactly, so a support that will not fit means a divider has moved. Use pocket holes drilled on their back faces so nothing shows inside the cabinet. Then run the two 20 1/4" front-to-back nailers across the top of the carcass; 20 1/4" is the full depth of the plywood parts, so they land flush front and back. Those nailers are what the countertop screws into, so put them where a driver can actually reach them once the top is on.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame flat on the bench: two 35" stiles outside, the two 45" top and bottom rails between them — 45" plus the two 1 1/2" stiles is the 48" the carcass already measures — plus the 45" kick rail, the two 22 3/4" door and drawer opening stiles, and the two 5 3/4" centre shelf face stiles. Join every joint with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" screws, checking the diagonals before you lift it off the bench, because a frame squared on a flat surface is far easier than one framed onto a standing box. Glue the finished frame to the carcass and pin it with 1 1/4" brads, keeping the stiles flush with the outside faces of the plywood sides. Then measure each finished opening and write the numbers down.

steps.title: Install the drawer slides
steps.body: Mount the Euro slides with screws — not nails and glue — to the divider and the cabinet side of the drawer bay, so they stay adjustable and removable. Set the two halves of the pair level with each other and square to the face frame, and follow the hardware's own instructions for setback from the front. Read the slide's required side clearance before you build the box, and note that these are 16" slides: the drawer box has to run at least 16" front to back to use them.

steps.title: Build the drawer box
steps.body: Measure the drawer opening you actually have, subtract the clearance your slides require on each side, and build the box to that. Do not build to the cut list here: it gives 5 1/2" drawer sides and a 4 3/4" x 17 3/4" bottom, which makes a box only about 5 3/4" deep front to back — nowhere near the 16" the specified slides need. Join the 1/2" stock with 1/2" pocket holes and 1" pocket hole screws, and pin the 1/4" plywood bottom on with 1 1/4" brads and glue. Check the box's diagonals before the glue sets; a racked box binds in its slides no matter how carefully the slides were mounted. Set the finished box back 3/4" from the face frame so the drawer front has somewhere to land.

steps.title: Build the door
steps.body: Build each shaker door from two 16" stiles and two 9 3/4" rails, joined with pocket holes and glue on the back face, then fit the 1/2" plywood panel. Dry-fit before gluing: the 2 1/2"-wide frame parts leave a 9 3/4" x 11" opening while the listed panel is 10 3/4" x 16", so the panel cannot sit inside the frame as listed and is meant to be applied to its back. Decide which you are building and size the finished door to the opening you measured rather than to the plan's numbers — plywood thickness and face frame reveal both shift the real opening.

steps.title: Fit the door and drawer front, add hardware
steps.body: Hang the door on the non-mortise hinges and shim it until the reveal around it is even top to bottom. Attach the drawer front from inside the box so no fastener shows, holding an even gap with shims while you drive the first two screws. Then add the knobs and pulls, marking their positions from a story stick rather than from each door's own edge so they line up across the whole vanity. Note that the summary describes one door and one drawer while the cut list and the hinge count describe two doors — build to whatever openings your face frame actually has.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every surface through the grits, easing the plywood edges and the face frame arrises. Fill the brad holes, sand them flush, and vacuum before finishing. Prime and paint, or stain and seal — and finish the inside of the cabinet and the underside of the centre shelf as well. A bathroom vanity lives in steam, and an unsealed plywood edge will swell and delaminate long before the painted face shows any wear.
materials: Paint, primer or stain and poly

images.alt: 48 inch bathroom vanity with an open center shelf
